title: Beyond GDP: a better way to measure national prosperity?
text:

One way that the inclusive wealth index (IWI) goes beyond the gross domestic product (GDP) is that it incorporates natural assets such as ecosystems.Credit: Twenty47studio/Moment/GettyFor almost a century, gross domestic product, or GDP, has been the common global measure for the total economic output of countries. It is calculated from the total monetary value of all final goods and services a nation produces over a period of time — but its limitations in measuring economic resilience and sustainability have long been recognized.Critics argue that GDP fails to adequately account for other kinds of ‘wealth’ in our societies such as human wellbeing, and the assets and costs that come from the natural environment. There have long been calls for broader metrics that address these shortcomings.“Every metric is imperfect, but that doesn’t mean we shouldn’t at least try to measure what matters,” says Shunsuke Managi, a member of the International Association of Traffic and Safety Sciences (IATSS) and an economist/engineer studying alternatives to GDP at Kyushu University in Fukuoka, Japan. Managi is also the director of the university’s Urban Institute.Shunsuke Managi of the International Association of Traffic and Safety Sciences (IATSS) is exploring the intersection between mobility systems and sustainability and human wellbeing.Credit: Kyushu UniversityManagi is working with the IATSS, an independent, interdisciplinary research body in Tokyo, Japan. IATSS was founded in 1974 by the co-founders of Honda Motor Company, Soichiro Honda and Takeo Fujisawa, in response to the rise in car and motorcycle use and increasing traffic fatalities. The association has achieved significant success in promoting well-being within the transportation sector through decades of research, practical social implementation, and policy recommendations.Managi and his IATSS colleagues are conducting research into ‘mobility systems’ and how they intersect with sustainability and human wellbeing. Mobility systems are the transport networks and services that facilitate movement of people and goods, and they form an important part of the overall picture of a nation’s output, such as GDP.The team is employing AI-driven analysis to generate very fine-grained metrics that map entire mobility supply chains to contribute to improved measures of economic output.Managi is one of the architects of the ‘inclusive wealth index’, or IWI, developed through United Nations-led research programmes as part of its ‘Beyond GDP’ initiative1. IWI is intended to offer a more balanced metric of national prosperity and wellbeing than GDP, as it incorporates environmental and social factors, in addition to manufacturing and other outputs, Managi explains.Moving metricsA simple concept underlies IWI: determining the true cost of a supply chain requires accounting for its assets, not just what it produces, as this shows whether a supply chain is creating lasting value or eroding the foundations it depends on. But applying that principle can be challenging. “No company truly understands its entire supply chain,” argues Managi, which is a core challenge in using these to build measures of total economic output.To help companies get a fuller picture of their supply chains, the IWI incorporates not just what is produced and sold, but also ‘human capital’, such as knowledge, education and skills; ‘manufactured capital’, such as roads, buildings and machinery; and ‘natural capital’, including forests, oceans, the atmosphere and ecosystems.These are factors that have often been overlooked or undervalued in international economics historically, and the IWI now offers a measure of productivity that accounts for the environmental and social impacts of economic output, rather than displacing these costs to other parts of a supply chain.The inclusive wealth index (IWI) is helpful for evaluating mobility systems such as trains and road vehicles.Credit: Ruben Earth/Moment/GettyAccounting for all elements of a supply chain is especially hard in the transport and mobility sector, where globalized supply chains make trade-offs difficult to track.A typical mobility system, such as a railway system or a road network, has a wide network of inputs. These range from raw-material extraction for things such as batteries and vehicles and the construction of infrastructure to energy generation and end-use of transport in cities. Each stage has its own environmental, social and economic costs and impacts.Capturing all of this requires a totally new system of analysis and measurement, and a broader vision of what mobility means.Transport systems and mobility “should not only move people — they should contribute to a better society as a whole,” says Managi, who is also the director of a tech startup that combines environmental, social, and governance (ESG) perspective with AI.AI assistanceManagi and the IATSS researchers are rethinking how mobility supply chains are measured from the ground up. Their work with AI to generate microlevel metrics considers three dimensions: well-being, sustainability and resilience.Well-being might examine whether a mobility supply chain supports local communities in emerging economies, or whether it carries risks such as forced labour. Sustainability goes beyond solely carbon emissions to also include costs such as environmental degradation and impacts on biodiversity. Resilience focuses on how supply chains can be strengthened to withstand geopolitical shifts and evolving international regulations.The AI is using company information, publicly available information and satellite data as its raw material. This allows the team to map impacts not just across sectors, but across geography and time. For example, a single transport project can be linked to upstream mineral extraction, labour conditions in processing hubs and environmental impacts along logistics routes.Through the power of AI tools, the team has been able to move from vague national averages to precise, spatial data “down to a grid with a 500-metre resolution,” says Managi. This level of granularity can help identify trade-offs that would otherwise be difficult to detect, such as cases where reductions in urban carbon emissions lead to environmental degradation or exploitative labour practices elsewhere in the supply chain.A resilient futureUltimately, Managi and his IATSS colleagues see mobility as a transdisciplinary science, comprised of mobility, sustainability and wellbeing. They also want to empower citizens in their decision making.“My goal is to turn this theory into practice. Not just academic ideas, but real implementation,” he says.He hopes this will make the IWI easier to understand and trust by showing real-world impacts and long-term outcomes — from better local air quality to improved global supply chains.The researchers are already attempting to make this happen through projects on the ground in Bangkok, Thailand and Delhi, India that are studying infrastructure, traffic congestion and air pollution.“The future of mobility is not a destination we reach,” says Managi. “It is a value we build, one transparent link at a time.”
Learn more about inclusive wealth and how it relates to transportation.

ReferencesManagi, S. & Kumar, P. (eds.) Inclusive Wealth Report 2018: Measuring Progress Towards Sustainability (1st ed.) Routledge (2018).
